22.07.25 10:42 – Customer response
It only affects the Basel location.
Both incoming and outgoing calls.
The disconnection occurs almost exactly after 30 seconds, sometimes slightly earlier.
Home office users are not affected.
22.07.25 11:30 – Internal note
Test performed with affected user.
External call to mobile phone.
Call disconnected after 29–31 seconds.
No error message in the Teams client, simply “Call Ended.”
23.07.25 08:55 – Initial hypothesis
Possible client error or unstable connection.
Test with another device → same behavior.
Test with LAN cable instead of docking station → no change.
23.07.25 11:10 – Email to customer
Good day
We are currently analyzing the Direct Routing logs.
Signaling is established correctly.
The disconnection does not appear to be triggered by the client.
Were there any recent network changes at the Basel location?
Kind regards
23.07.25 13:45 – Customer response
On Friday, a new network segment for VoIP traffic was implemented.
A separate VLAN for “Voice” was created.
Could this be relevant?
24.07.25 09:05 – Internal analysis
SBC logs reviewed:
– SIP INVITE successful
– 200 OK correct
– Call established
– After 30 seconds, BYE from SBC
No obvious error code.
24.07.25 10:30 – Firewall analysis
No blocking visible.
Ports open according to standard configuration.
24.07.25 14:20 – Detailed SIP analysis
Session-Expires header reviewed.
Session timer set to 30 seconds.
Comparison with other locations shows:
There, 180 seconds.
25.07.25 08:50 – Review of internal documentation
Document:
“Blueprint – Microsoft Teams Direct Routing & Session Handling – Version 2.1”
Section on NAT session persistence and SIP session timers relevant.
Note in the blueprint:
For certain firewall or NAT configurations, the session timer must be adjusted to avoid premature termination.
25.07.25 11:15 – Internal tests
Temporary adjustment of session parameters on SBC.
Test call performed.
Call remains stable for several minutes.
Reverting to original configuration → disconnection after approx. 30 seconds reproducible.
28.07.25 – Coordination with network team
New “Voice” VLAN uses different NAT policy.
UDP session timeout configured to 30 seconds.
Comparison with Zurich location → 180 seconds.
29.07.25 – Adjustment
NAT session timeout adjusted according to architecture specification.
Details not documented in ticket for security reasons.

Good morning

Thank you very much.
31.07.25 15:52 – Ticket closure

Calls now remain stable.
Kind regards
Consulting Sample Company
is_rec_reason: This is a descriptive observation about session/timeout timing and alignment, not a recommendation or directive proposing an action.

Good morning

12.08.25 11:40 – Internal analysis

• Presence displayed as “Offline”
• Chat delivered
• Status does not change

Get-CsTenantFederationConfiguration
Observation:
AllowPublicUsers = False
AllowFederatedUsers = True

• Difference between Federation and Presence Sharing
• Impact of Privacy Mode
• Behavior with restrictive policies
• Required settings for partners using Direct Routing

Presence displayed correctly.
Reverted to production configuration →
External presence again permanently “Offline”.

Configuration adjusted according to Federation Blueprint.
Details not documented in ticket for security reasons.

External partners are again correctly displayed as “Available” or “Busy”.
Chats arrive without delay.
Calls are signaled again.

The root cause was a configuration deviation in the area of Federation and Presence Sharing following a policy adjustment.
The configuration was corrected according to the internal Teams Federation architecture.
Presence display is now functioning correctly again.

Consulting Sample Company

18.12.24 00:34 Re: TRAIN-CE/FO integration / MFA
Dear Employee 7,
During the joint analysis of the issue, we identified that two parameters were not correctly set during the ADFS client registration. In the new client application used specifically for the MFA request of the Toolbox on the Train system, the following parameters were set to true:
EnableJWT
RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders

EnableJWT activates the issuance of JSON Web Tokens in the authentication process. If enabled, ADFS issues JWTs instead of other token formats such as SAML. These tokens are evaluated by the target application. If the target system does not support JWTs or cannot validate their signature, authentication fails.

RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders forwards MFA requirements to configured claims providers. This delegates MFA execution to external providers and increases complexity in authentication flow, logging, and auditing.

The combination of these parameters can lead to login failures if the target system is not designed to handle JWTs or external MFA claims.

We have documented the configuration to avoid future issues. From our perspective, no further problems are expected after adjusting the parameters. If MFA works stably and the Train environment is reachable again, the login topic can be considered resolved.

Kind regards
Employee 21

REPORT 10:
# Customer Company 52 AG — Teams Direct Routing Incident (Basel Voice VLAN & SBC Translation)

A production telephony incident (KF52 / TI-1612036) impacted Customer Company 52 AG’s incoming PSTN calls after a carrier migration of a new number range and a recent Voice VLAN deployment at the Basel site. Two simultaneous, independent failure modes produced the observed symptoms: (1) missing translation/normalization entries for the new number range in the SBC translation table caused INVITEs to be mis‑answered with 404 and prevented routing to Teams; and (2) a site-specific NAT/UDP session timeout applied to the new Voice VLAN (30s) caused SIP sessions to be torn down (~29–31s BYE from the SBC) and produced one‑way audio in some traces. Diagnosis used SBC logs, Teams Admin Center, and reproducible test calls; remediation combined a permanent update to the SBC routing/translation rules and aligning the Basel NAT/session timeout with the Direct Routing blueprint, after which incoming calls were confirmed functional and the ticket was closed. [Data: Entities (171, 60); Relationships (257, 532)]

## Incident scope and customer impact

The support ticket KF52 (TI-1612036) documents a production-impacting PSTN-to-Teams telephony problem opened for Customer Company 52 AG and explicitly cites affected example numbers, user reports, and the Basel site as the place where the symptoms were observed. The ticket lifecycle included customer-provided example numbers and timeline information used to reproduce and validate fixes [Data: Entities (171, 172, 60); Relationships (257, 258)].

## Root cause A — Missing SBC translation entries for the new number range produced 404 mis-answers

SBC logs and configuration artifacts showed that calls in the newly provisioned number range were not present in the SBC translation/normalization tables and thus were mis-answered (404 Not Found) instead of being forwarded to Teams. The new number range had been provisioned by the carrier but was omitted from the SBC translation table and routing rules until a temporary translation rule validated the fix and a permanent update was applied [Data: Entities (178, 183, 184); Relationships (276, 275, 266)].

## Root cause B — Voice VLAN NAT/UDP session timeout (30s) caused early BYE and one‑way audio

SBC call traces repeatedly showed a BYE originating from the SBC at ~29–31 seconds terminating otherwise-successful INVITE/200 OK sessions; parallel network configuration review identified the Basel Voice VLAN’s NAT/UDP session timeout set to 30 seconds (versus 180s in Zurich). This mismatch in NAT/session persistence caused session state to expire prematurely and, in some traces, resulted in client‑to‑SBC RTP not being consistently visible (one‑way audio). Adjusting the UDP session timeout to align with the Direct Routing blueprint eliminated the premature disconnects in post-change testing [Data: Entities (334, 324, 326, 325, 440); Relationships (524, 530, 536)].

## Diagnostic evidence and tooling supported reproducible root-cause isolation

Investigators used SBC LOGS and Teams Admin Center diagnostics alongside reproducible test calls to isolate signaling vs media failures: SIP signaling (INVITE/200 OK) was present while RTP from internal clients was intermittently absent, and test calls consistently reproduced the ~30s disconnect and client 'Call Ended' symptom. These artifacts (SBC logs, SIP message traces, Teams telemetry, and test calls) provided the evidence chain to separate the translation/routing fault from the media/NAT fault and to validate each remediation step [Data: Entities (182, 175, 323, 330); Relationships (524, 734, 544)].

## Remediation steps taken and engineering coordination

The support engineer (Employee 41) coordinated with the carrier, applied a temporary translation rule to validate inclusion of the new number range, and then applied permanent routing/translation updates; the Network/Unified Communications teams adjusted the Voice VLAN NAT timeout to align with the Direct Routing blueprint and applied relevant firewall/NAT policy changes. Temporary SBC parameter adjustments were used during testing to confirm the session timer/NAT hypothesis before committing permanent changes [Data: Entities (173, 186, 329, 328, 326); Relationships (266, 267, 527, 520)].

## Operational ownership and inter-team handoffs were clear but cross-team playbooks are implied

Ownership during the incident involved Support (ticket owner), Employee 41 (troubleshooting and change execution), the Unified Communications / Network Team for SBC and media flow, and the Network Team for VLAN and NAT policy changes; the carrier provided confirmation of delivery to the SIP trunk which helped narrow remediation scope. While the teams performed the necessary coordination, the incident indicates a need for a formalized handoff checklist for number-range provisioning, SBC change validation, and site VLAN configuration to reduce friction on future events [Data: Entities (113, 141, 177, 173); Relationships (178, 274, 266, 1118)].

## Residual risks and policy gaps

Two policy/process gaps could permit recurrence: (1) lack of a documented, automated step to propagate new carrier number ranges into SBC translation/routing rules and voice-routing policies, and (2) inconsistent NAT/session timeout profiles across sites for Voice VLANs. These gaps risk future mis‑routing or session drops when carriers change assignments or when new voice VLANs are deployed, and both are addressed in the Direct Routing blueprint but require operational controls to enforce across sites [Data: Entities (179, 328, 325); Relationships (278, 520, 532)].

## Priority runbook and monitoring recommendations

Create and enforce a short, prioritized runbook: (a) include a mandatory translation-table update step whenever carriers provision new number ranges (validate with temporary rule and test calls), (b) ensure Voice VLAN NAT/session timeouts match blueprint-recommended values (e.g., 180s) before cutover, (c) add post-change validation with test calls and Teams Admin Center checks for incoming call activity, and (d) add telemetry/alerts for elevated BYE frequency and asymmetric RTP visibility to detect early regressions. These measures map directly to artifacts and test evidence used in the incident response and will materially reduce time-to-detect and time-to-recover on recurrence [Data: Entities (186, 330, 175, 328); Relationships (267, 544, 734, 520)].

REPORT 5:
# KF52 (Customer Company 52 AG) — Microsoft Teams Direct Routing / Zurich Media Bypass Incident

This community centers on Customer Company 52 AG’s Microsoft Teams telephony estate and adjacent operational controls: Teams (Direct Routing), the Zurich office network/firewall, Site-level Media Bypass configuration, SBC translation/numbering, and supporting operational blueprints and runbooks. The outage pattern comprised external PSTN calls with one-way audio or disconnection after ~30 seconds caused by a weekend security hardening firewall change at the Zurich site that restricted outbound RTP/media port ranges required by Direct Routing in Media Bypass mode. Troubleshooting followed the internal Blueprints (Direct Routing & Media Flow v2.1 and Direct Routing & Number Translation v2.3), involved helpdesk owner Employee 33 and System Engineer 18, and required coordination between UC/Direct Routing, network/firewall teams and external consultants. Several related but distinct user-impact tickets (camera not detected, notifications not displayed, microphone not connected) were resolved via standard client/OS permission checks and hardware verification per device/permissions blueprints. The event exposed gaps in change validation for site-specific firewall rules, the operational impact of Media Bypass at the site edge, and an AD/GPO membership error that created unintended printer mappings during a reorganization.

## Root cause: Zurich outbound firewall change blocked required RTP/media ports and caused one-way audio and short call sessions

The primary failure mode was a weekend security hardening change at the Zurich site that introduced a more restrictive outbound firewall rule which did not permit the full RTP/media port ranges required for Teams Direct Routing, producing one-way audio and call disconnects at ~30 seconds until corrected.

This causal chain (firewall change → blocked media ports → media-only failures while signaling completed) is documented in the incident ticket analysis and the relationships tying the TI-1602241 investigation to the firewall change and to the Zurich firewall rule specifics. This finding is supported by ticket records and configuration review that noted the Zurich rule deviated from the Direct Routing/media blueprint and that correcting the rule restored voice transmission stability. [Data: Entities (116,126,61); Relationships (172,184,186,721)]

## Site Media Bypass configuration amplified impact of the firewall change

Zurich was configured with Media Bypass, meaning client RTP streams were sent directly to the SBC rather than via a media relay; as a result, outbound port availability at the site edge became critical and the restrictive outbound rule immediately impacted audio media while signaling (SIP) could still appear functional for some sessions. Because Media Bypass places media flow responsibility on the site edge, any deviation from the port ranges or NAT traversal expectations in the Direct Routing blueprint will produce the observed asymmetric audio behavior.

This amplification due to Media Bypass is documented in the blueprint/setting references and in the relationship linking the Zurich location to Media Bypass and to the blueprint validation steps used by engineers. [Data: Entities (445,129,115); Relationships (745,177,181)]

## Authoritative blueprints were used effectively but documentation gaps and deviations contributed to the fault and a separate numbering oversight

Operations relied on the internal Blueprints (Direct Routing & Media Flow v2.1 and Direct Routing & Number Translation v2.3) to validate expected media port ranges, NAT traversal behavior, and SBC translation rules; these documents were the specification used to identify the firewall deviation and to implement the corrective configuration. However, the customer’s internal documentation around number migrations was out of date, which separately contributed to an SBC translation-table oversight during a carrier number migration.

Evidence of blueprint-led remediation and the documentation gap appear in the ticket and relationship records: engineers compared the Zurich firewall against blueprint port ranges, applied fixes per the blueprint, and separately traced a translation-table omission to outdated internal docs. [Data: Entities (115,174,187); Relationships (181,270,265)]

## Cross-functional incident response and escalation chain were exercised (helpdesk → UC specialist → system engineer → network/firewall)

Initial triage and client-level checks were performed by Employee 33 (helpdesk/owner), who escalated complex Direct Routing/media-flow issues to BU Unified Communications and System Engineer 18 for SBC and firewall analysis and coordination with the network/firewall team. This escalation path enabled the deeper analysis of SBC logs, firewall rules and the eventual configuration correction performed according to the Direct Routing architecture.

Ticket ownership and the escalation path are recorded in the ticket activity and relationship graph that link Employee 33 to System Engineer 18, TI-1602241 to BU Unified Communications, and System Engineer 18 to the firewall configuration action. [Data: Entities (110,111,438,107); Relationships (174,717,721)]

## Temporary test changes confirmed the hypothesis but a rollback/reversion risk existed until permanent correction

A temporary firewall adjustment that relaxed outbound media port restrictions restored bidirectional audio in tests, which validated the firewall as the root cause. That temporary change was later reverted to the restrictive rule, reproducing the problem until the permanent correction—applied per the blueprint—was made and validated with a 48-hour monitoring window.

The sequence of temporary adjustment → restored audio → reversion → permanent correction and monitoring is recorded in the firewall change and ticket relationships and closure notes. [Data: Entities (128,449,116); Relationships (189,719,186)]

## Multiple distinct, lower-severity client/device issues were present and handled via device/permission blueprints

Alongside the network-level media outage, several user-facing tickets were unrelated to the firewall change and were resolved using client/OS troubleshooting per established blueprints: a camera-not-detected ticket was resolved by adjusting OS app/camera permissions, a notifications-not-displayed ticket was resolved by enabling the Teams notification channel and adjusting Windows Focus Assist, and an isolated microphone case was a hardware connection issue (microphone cable) that required reconnection.

These separate fixes reference device/permissions blueprints and are recorded in their respective tickets and blueprint-relationship links, demonstrating correct use of client-focused runbooks for non-network faults. [Data: Entities (735,377,666,737); Relationships (1317,614,1177,1320)]

## AD/GPO membership change caused unintended printer mapping, indicating governance risk in group membership controls

A reorganization that placed the Finance team into a new AD group inadvertently made that new Finance AD group a member of the higher-level Zurich location AD group. As a result, Zurich-targeted Group Policy (including the default printer MFP-ZUERICH-01) applied to Finance users in other locations, producing an unintended printer default assignment until corrected. This shows a governance/control gap where nested AD group membership was not validated against location-targeted GPOs.

The AD/GPO misconfiguration and its effect on default printer assignment are documented in the AD group and GPO relationship records and the affected user testing/validation tickets. [Data: Entities (55,56,51); Relationships (87,90,77)]

## Operational monitoring, post-change validation and runbook adherence provided effective closure but change-validation controls should be strengthened

The remediation was validated by monitoring (48 hours without anomalies) and ticket closure notes that referenced applying the Direct Routing architecture/blueprints. This indicates that when blueprints are followed and monitoring is in place, service stability can be restored. However, the initial outbound firewall change that caused the incident suggests the change-control process did not adequately validate site-specific Direct Routing/Media Bypass implications (port ranges, NAT behavior), and the temporary-reversion episode highlights the need for controlled test windows and rollback coordination.

Monitoring and blueprint usage are recorded in the remediation artifacts and ticket closures; these records support strengthening pre-change validation, test/rollback planning, and mandatory blueprint conformance checks for site-level firewall changes. [Data: Entities (449,115,116); Relationships (719,181,186)]

18.12.24 00:34 Re: TRAIN-CE/FO integration / MFA
Dear Employee 7,
During the joint analysis of the issue, we identified that two parameters were not correctly set during the ADFS client registration. In the new client application used specifically for the MFA request of the Toolbox on the Train system, the following parameters were set to true:
EnableJWT
RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders

EnableJWT activates the issuance of JSON Web Tokens in the authentication process. If enabled, ADFS issues JWTs instead of other token formats such as SAML. These tokens are evaluated by the target application. If the target system does not support JWTs or cannot validate their signature, authentication fails.

RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders forwards MFA requirements to configured claims providers. This delegates MFA execution to external providers and increases complexity in authentication flow, logging, and auditing.

The combination of these parameters can lead to login failures if the target system is not designed to handle JWTs or external MFA claims.

We have documented the configuration to avoid future issues. From our perspective, no further problems are expected after adjusting the parameters. If MFA works stably and the Train environment is reachable again, the login topic can be considered resolved.

Kind regards
Employee 21

18.12.24 00:34 Re: TRAIN-CE/FO integration / MFA
Dear Employee 7,
During the joint analysis of the issue, we identified that two parameters were not correctly set during the ADFS client registration. In the new client application used specifically for the MFA request of the Toolbox on the Train system, the following parameters were set to true:
EnableJWT
RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders

EnableJWT activates the issuance of JSON Web Tokens in the authentication process. If enabled, ADFS issues JWTs instead of other token formats such as SAML. These tokens are evaluated by the target application. If the target system does not support JWTs or cannot validate their signature, authentication fails.

RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders forwards MFA requirements to configured claims providers. This delegates MFA execution to external providers and increases complexity in authentication flow, logging, and auditing.

The combination of these parameters can lead to login failures if the target system is not designed to handle JWTs or external MFA claims.

We have documented the configuration to avoid future issues. From our perspective, no further problems are expected after adjusting the parameters. If MFA works stably and the Train environment is reachable again, the login topic can be considered resolved.

Kind regards
Employee 21

18.09.25 08:40 – Email from Contact Person 22

Callers report that they either receive a busy tone or the connection is disconnected after a few seconds.

Ticket created and assigned to Employee 41.
18.09.25 09:25 – Helpdesk inquiry
Good day

– Example phone numbers where the issue occurs

18.09.25 10:15 – Customer response
Here are two examples:
+41 44 785 23 10
+41 44 785 23 11
Other numbers such as +41 31 … work without issue.
The problem has existed since last week.
All employees are affected.
18.09.25 11:40 – Internal analysis
Test call from private mobile (different number range) → works.
Test with number in specified range → call not delivered.
In the Teams Admin Center, no incoming call is shown.
19.09.25 09:05 – Initial hypothesis
Possible provider issue.
Carrier contacted.
19.09.25 14:20 – Carrier feedback
From the carrier’s perspective, the call is correctly delivered to the company’s SBC.
No routing errors visible on their side.
22.09.25 08:55 – SBC log analysis
For functioning numbers:
INVITE accepted and forwarded to Teams.
For affected numbers:
INVITE received but immediately answered with “404 Not Found”.
22.09.25 11:30 – Email to customer
Good day
We see that incoming calls from the provider are reaching the SBC.
However, the requests are not being correctly forwarded internally.
Were there recent changes to number ranges or new extensions?
Kind regards
22.09.25 13:05 – Customer response
Yes, we received a new number range from the provider last week and partially migrated old numbers.
The affected numbers belong to this new range.
23.09.25 09:10 – Internal review
Comparison with document
“Blueprint – Microsoft Teams Direct Routing & Number Translation – Version 2.3”
Section regarding:
• Normalization of incoming numbers
• E.164 format
• Translation rules on SBC
• Voice routing policies
Finding:
New numbers are not included in the SBC translation table.
Routing rule covers only the old number range.
24.09.25 – Test
Temporary adjustment of translation rule for new number range.
Test call performed.
Call correctly signaled and delivered.
Reverting to original configuration → issue reproducible.
25.09.25 – Coordination with customer
Confirmation that new number range was fully migrated.
Internal documentation had not yet been updated.
26.09.25 – Adjustment
Routing and translation rules extended according to architectural specification.
Details not documented in ticket for security reasons.
29.09.25 – Customer feedback
All tested numbers are working again.
Multiple customers also confirmed successful calls.
Thank you for the support.
29.09.25 16:18 – Ticket closure
Dear Contact Person 22
The root cause was an inconsistent translation and routing configuration following the introduction of a new number range.
The configuration was adjusted according to the internal Direct Routing architecture.
Incoming calls are now correctly delivered again.
Kind regards
Consulting Sample Company

18.12.24 00:34 Re: TRAIN-CE/FO integration / MFA
Dear Employee 7,
During the joint analysis of the issue, we identified that two parameters were not correctly set during the ADFS client registration. In the new client application used specifically for the MFA request of the Toolbox on the Train system, the following parameters were set to true:
EnableJWT
RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders

EnableJWT activates the issuance of JSON Web Tokens in the authentication process. If enabled, ADFS issues JWTs instead of other token formats such as SAML. These tokens are evaluated by the target application. If the target system does not support JWTs or cannot validate their signature, authentication fails.

RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders forwards MFA requirements to configured claims providers. This delegates MFA execution to external providers and increases complexity in authentication flow, logging, and auditing.

The combination of these parameters can lead to login failures if the target system is not designed to handle JWTs or external MFA claims.

We have documented the configuration to avoid future issues. From our perspective, no further problems are expected after adjusting the parameters. If MFA works stably and the Train environment is reachable again, the login topic can be considered resolved.

Kind regards
Employee 21

18.12.24 00:34 Re: TRAIN-CE/FO integration / MFA
Dear Employee 7,
During the joint analysis of the issue, we identified that two parameters were not correctly set during the ADFS client registration. In the new client application used specifically for the MFA request of the Toolbox on the Train system, the following parameters were set to true:
EnableJWT
RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders

EnableJWT activates the issuance of JSON Web Tokens in the authentication process. If enabled, ADFS issues JWTs instead of other token formats such as SAML. These tokens are evaluated by the target application. If the target system does not support JWTs or cannot validate their signature, authentication fails.

RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders forwards MFA requirements to configured claims providers. This delegates MFA execution to external providers and increases complexity in authentication flow, logging, and auditing.

The combination of these parameters can lead to login failures if the target system is not designed to handle JWTs or external MFA claims.

We have documented the configuration to avoid future issues. From our perspective, no further problems are expected after adjusting the parameters. If MFA works stably and the Train environment is reachable again, the login topic can be considered resolved.

Kind regards
Employee 21

18.09.25 08:40 – Email from Contact Person 22
Good morning

Kind regards
18.09.25 08:56
Ticket created and assigned to Employee 41.
18.09.25 09:25 – Helpdesk inquiry
Good day

– Example phone numbers where the issue occurs

– Whether all users are affected or only specific ones

+41 44 785 23 10
+41 44 785 23 11

The problem has existed since last week.
All employees are affected.
18.09.25 11:40 – Internal analysis
Test call from private mobile (different number range) → works.

In the Teams Admin Center, no incoming call is shown.
19.09.25 09:05 – Initial hypothesis
Possible provider issue.
Carrier contacted.
19.09.25 14:20 – Carrier feedback
From the carrier’s perspective, the call is correctly delivered to the company’s SBC.

22.09.25 08:55 – SBC log analysis
For functioning numbers:
INVITE accepted and forwarded to Teams.
For affected numbers:
INVITE received but immediately answered with “404 Not Found”.
22.09.25 11:30 – Email to customer
Good day
We see that incoming calls from the provider are reaching the SBC.
However, the requests are not being correctly forwarded internally.
Were there recent changes to number ranges or new extensions?
Kind regards

Yes, we received a new number range from the provider last week and partially migrated old numbers.
The affected numbers belong to this new range.
23.09.25 09:10 – Internal review
Comparison with document
“Blueprint – Microsoft Teams Direct Routing & Number Translation – Version 2.3”
Section regarding:
• Normalization of incoming numbers
• E.164 format
• Translation rules on SBC
• Voice routing policies
Finding:
New numbers are not included in the SBC translation table.
Routing rule covers only the old number range.
24.09.25 – Test
Temporary adjustment of translation rule for new number range.
Test call performed.
Call correctly signaled and delivered.
Reverting to original configuration → issue reproducible.
25.09.25 – Coordination with customer
Confirmation that new number range was fully migrated.
Internal documentation had not yet been updated.
26.09.25 – Adjustment
Routing and translation rules extended according to architectural specification.
Details not documented in ticket for security reasons.
29.09.25 – Customer feedback
All tested numbers are working again.
Multiple customers also confirmed successful calls.
Thank you for the support.
29.09.25 16:18 – Ticket closure
Dear Contact Person 22
The root cause was an inconsistent translation and routing configuration following the introduction of a new number range.
The configuration was adjusted according to the internal Direct Routing architecture.
Incoming calls are now correctly delivered again.
Kind regards
Consulting Sample Company
